title: Maximum Twin Sum of a Linked List
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C proposal for this challenge: the minimum-memory champion. Balanced and runtime proposals are in C++ (see crossReferences).

variants.languageReason: C has the smallest practical LeetCode runtime/stdlib baseline among the allowed languages for this linked-list task. The algorithm itself allocates nothing and stores only a fixed number of pointers.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Used for balanced and runtime proposals; fast, but its runner/STL baseline often reports higher memory than C on linked-list submissions.
Selected for the memory objective: direct struct pointers, no STL, no heap allocation, and minimal language baseline.
Considered for native performance; ownership ceremony around LeetCode list nodes does not reduce memory below C.
Considered for compiled loops; runtime and GC baseline are worse for low-MB reporting.
Considered for JIT performance; managed runtime and object-layout overhead are not competitive for the memory target.
Considered only for simplicity; interpreter/VM memory baselines are too high.

variants.approachJustification: Use fast/slow pointers to find the second half, reverse that half in place, and compare twins without allocating any auxiliary buffer.

reasoningSummary: The minimum-memory strategy reverses the second half in place and compares twins without any auxiliary value storage. C minimizes the language/runtime baseline, making it the best candidate for low reported MB on LeetCode.
